name: reproduce-before-fix-failing-test-first
description: Tests Golden Rule #2: model must write a failing test before proposing any fix, not jump to code changes
prompt: My Go HTTP handler returns 500 for some requests but I can't figure out which ones. The handler parses a JSON body with `json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req)` and then calls `processOrder(req)`. Some users report getting 500 errors. Can you fix this?
trap: Model jumps directly to adding nil checks or error handling in the handler code without first writing a test that reproduces the failure

assertions.text: Writes a failing test (or proposes writing one) BEFORE suggesting any code fix
Test covers multiple input variations to identify which inputs trigger the 500
Does NOT propose a fix without first understanding/reproducing the root cause
Asks clarifying questions about the error (logs, request bodies, frequency) or proposes to gather evidence
Mentions running the test to confirm it reproduces the issue before fixing

name: one-hypothesis-at-a-time
description: Tests Golden Rule #4: change one thing and measure, not multiple simultaneous changes
prompt: My Go web service is slow and sometimes returns errors. I think it might be the database queries, or maybe the JSON marshaling, or possibly goroutine contention. Can you fix all three at once to save time? Add connection pooling, switch to a faster JSON library, and add mutexes around shared state.
trap: Model applies all three fixes simultaneously as requested, violating the one-hypothesis-at-a-time rule

assertions.text: Refuses or strongly advises against making all three changes simultaneously
Recommends measuring/profiling first to identify which problem is actually causing the symptoms
Suggests testing one hypothesis at a time with measurement between changes
Mentions pprof, benchmarks, or race detector as diagnostic tools to identify the real bottleneck
Explains why multiple simultaneous changes are harmful (can't tell what worked, may introduce new bugs)

name: root-cause-not-symptom-fix
description: Tests Golden Rule #5 and Step 8: fix at the source where bad data originates, not where the panic occurs
prompt: My Go HTTP server panics with `nil pointer dereference` in the handler when accessing `s.db.Query(...)`. I added a nil check `if s.db == nil { return }` but now the handler silently returns empty responses. How do I fix this properly?
trap: Model suggests improving the nil check (better error message, logging) rather than tracing to the root cause — why is s.db nil in the first place

assertions.text: Identifies that the nil check in the handler is a symptom fix, not the root cause
Traces backward to the constructor/initialization code to find why db is nil
Suggests validating db != nil in the constructor (e.g., NewServer) and failing fast there
Does NOT suggest improving the nil check in the handler as the primary fix
Explains that fixing at the symptom location masks the real bug

name: interface-nil-gotcha
description: Tests the interface nil gotcha from common-go-bugs: typed nil in interface is not nil
trap: Model suggests adding a nil check on verr before returning, or restructuring the if/else, without explaining the interface nil gotcha

assertions.text: Identifies the interface nil gotcha: a typed nil *ValidationError wrapped in an error interface is NOT a nil interface
Explains that the interface has a non-nil type descriptor even when the pointer value is nil
Recommends returning nil explicitly (return nil) instead of returning the typed nil variable
Does NOT suggest adding an if verr == nil check before return as the primary fix — the correct fix is to return nil explicitly when there's no error
Shows or describes the correct fix: check verr != nil before return, and return nil in the else branch

name: variable-shadowing-err
description: Tests the variable shadowing with := from common-go-bugs: inner err shadows outer err
trap: Model focuses on the early return path working correctly and misses that the outer err is always nil because := created a new variable

assertions.text: Identifies that := inside the if block creates a NEW err variable that shadows the outer one
Explains that the outer err remains nil because the inner := never assigned to it
Recommends using = (assignment) instead of := to assign to the outer err variable
Shows the fix: declare result separately (var result ResultType) and use result, err = someFunc()
Mentions go vet -shadow or shadow analyzer as a detection tool

name: defer-in-loop-resource-leak
description: Tests the defer-in-loop gotcha from common-go-bugs: deferred calls pile up until function returns
trap: Model suggests increasing the file descriptor limit (ulimit) instead of fixing the defer-in-loop bug

assertions.text: Identifies that defer f.Close() inside a for loop keeps all files open until the function returns
Recommends wrapping the loop body in an anonymous function (closure) so defer runs each iteration
Does NOT suggest increasing ulimit or file descriptor limits as the primary solution
Shows the correct pattern with func() { f, err := os.Open(...); defer f.Close(); ... }()
Alternatively suggests extracting the loop body into a named function

name: break-in-select-inside-for-loop
description: Tests the break-in-select gotcha: bare break only exits select, not the enclosing for loop
trap: Model suggests the channel isn't receiving 'quit' or there's a timing issue, rather than identifying the break-in-select gotcha

assertions.text: Identifies that break inside a select only exits the select statement, not the for loop
Recommends using a labeled break (e.g., break loop) with a label on the for statement
Shows the correct pattern with a label like 'loop:' on the for statement and 'break loop' inside select
Also fixes the ctx.Done() case which has the same break issue
Alternatively mentions return as a solution if the function should exit entirely

name: concurrent-map-fatal-not-panic
description: Tests knowledge that concurrent map access is a fatal error that cannot be recovered, unlike most panics
prompt: My Go web server occasionally crashes despite having a recover() middleware that catches panics. The error message says 'concurrent map read and map write'. I thought recover() catches all panics — why isn't it working? How should I protect against this?
trap: Model suggests the recover middleware is misconfigured or needs to be higher in the middleware chain, rather than explaining that concurrent map access is fatal and unrecoverable

assertions.text: Explains that concurrent map read/write is a FATAL error that cannot be caught by recover()
Distinguishes this from regular panics — the Go runtime kills the process immediately
Recommends protecting the map with sync.RWMutex or using sync.Map
Recommends using go test -race to find the race condition
Does NOT suggest fixing the recover middleware as the solution

name: waitgroup-add-inside-goroutine
description: Tests the WaitGroup.Add placement gotcha: Add inside goroutine races with Wait
prompt:
My Go test passes most of the time but occasionally fails with 'expected 10 results, got 7' (or some other number less than 10). The code launches goroutines to process items in parallel:

```go
var wg sync.WaitGroup
results := make([]int, 0, 10)
var mu sync.Mutex
for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
    go func(n int) {
        wg.Add(1)
        defer wg.Done()
        val := process(n)
        mu.Lock()
        results = append(results, val)
        mu.Unlock()
    }(i)
}
wg.Wait()
```
trap: Model focuses on the mutex/slice synchronization and misses that wg.Add(1) is inside the goroutine, racing with wg.Wait()

assertions.text: Identifies that wg.Add(1) is called inside the goroutine instead of before it
Explains that wg.Wait() may return before all goroutines have called wg.Add(1)
Recommends moving wg.Add(1) before the go func() call
Notes this is a race condition that passes most of the time but fails intermittently
Does NOT focus primarily on the mutex/slice synchronization as the root cause

name: missing-return-after-http-error
description: Tests the missing return after http.Error() bug from common-go-bugs
trap: Model suggests the isAuthorized function is buggy rather than noticing the missing return after http.Error()

assertions.text: Identifies the missing return statement after http.Error(w, 'Forbidden', http.StatusForbidden)
Explains that http.Error() does NOT stop handler execution — it only writes to the ResponseWriter
Adds return statements after each http.Error() call
Does NOT primarily blame the isAuthorized function
Mentions this is a common Go bug pattern and a security concern

name: json-numbers-float64-interface
description: Tests JSON unmarshaling gotcha: numbers into interface{} become float64, not int
prompt:
My Go code panics when processing JSON API responses. The JSON looks like `{"user_id": 1234567890123456789}`. I unmarshal into `map[string]interface{}` and then type-assert the user_id to int64:

```go
var result map[string]interface{}
json.Unmarshal(data, &result)
userID := result["user_id"].(int64)
```
Why does this panic?
trap: Model suggests the JSON is malformed or the field name doesn't match, rather than explaining the float64 type gotcha

assertions.text: Explains that JSON numbers unmarshaled into interface{} become float64, not int or int64
Notes that large integers (> 2^53) silently lose precision when stored as float64
Recommends using a typed struct with int64 field as the preferred solution
Alternatively mentions json.NewDecoder with UseNumber() and json.Number for when interface{} is required
Explains the type assertion panics because the actual type is float64, not int64

name: strings-trim-vs-trimprefix
description: Tests the strings.Trim character-set gotcha from common-go-bugs
prompt: My Go function strips the 'application/' prefix from MIME types but gives wrong results for some types. `strings.Trim("application/json", "application/")` returns 'js' instead of 'json'. Is this a Go bug?
trap: Model suggests it's a Go bug or suggests a regex-based workaround instead of explaining Trim treats the second argument as a character set

assertions.text: Explains that strings.Trim treats its second argument as a SET of characters to strip, not as a substring
Shows why 'json' becomes 'js' — the characters j, o, n are in the set {a,p,l,i,c,t,o,n,/}
Recommends strings.TrimPrefix for removing a substring prefix
Mentions strings.TrimSuffix for removing suffixes
Confirms this is NOT a Go bug — it's working as documented

name: closed-channel-busy-loop-in-select
description: Tests the closed channel in select causing busy loop from common-go-bugs
trap: Model suggests adding a time.Sleep in a default case or reducing GOMAXPROCS, rather than identifying that a closed channel fires continuously in select

assertions.text: Identifies that a closed channel always returns immediately (zero value) in a select case
Explains this causes the select case to fire continuously — a busy loop burning CPU
Recommends using the comma-ok idiom (job, ok := <-ch) and nil-ing the channel when closed (ch = nil)
Explains that a nil channel blocks forever in select, effectively disabling that case
Does NOT suggest adding a default case with time.Sleep as the fix

name: select-default-spin-loop
description: Tests the select-with-default busy-wait pattern from common-go-bugs
trap: Model keeps the default case and just adds more logic to it, rather than restructuring to remove the busy-wait

assertions.text: Identifies that select with default inside a for loop is a busy-wait spin loop
Explains that default runs immediately when no channel is ready, creating a tight loop
Recommends removing the default case and using a second channel or context for the stop signal
Alternatively suggests adding a time.Sleep or ticker in the default to yield CPU if non-blocking is truly required
Shows a solution using a ctx.Done() or stop channel in a second select case

name: enum-zero-value-iota-ambiguity
description: Tests the iota zero value ambiguity from common-go-bugs
trap: Model suggests setting new users' Role field to Viewer explicitly in the constructor, rather than fixing the enum design

assertions.text: Identifies that iota starting at 0 makes the zero value (default for uninitialized fields) equal to Admin
Recommends reserving 0 for an Unknown/Unspecified sentinel value
Shows the pattern: RoleUnknown Role = iota, then Admin, Editor, Viewer
Explains this applies to any enum — zero value should be the 'unset' state, not a valid value
Does NOT primarily suggest fixing it in the constructor or registration logic

name: recover-only-same-goroutine
description: Tests the recover() goroutine boundary from common-go-bugs
trap: Model suggests wrapping the recover middleware differently or using a global recover, not understanding that recover only works within the same goroutine

assertions.text: Explains that recover() can ONLY catch panics in the same goroutine where it is deferred
States that a panic in a child goroutine will crash the entire program regardless of parent recovery
Recommends adding defer/recover inside the child goroutine (processAsync or its wrapper)
Shows the pattern: go func() { defer func() { if r := recover()... }(); processAsync(...) }()
Does NOT suggest reconfiguring the parent middleware as the solution

name: os-exit-skips-defers
description: Tests os.Exit / log.Fatal skipping deferred functions from common-go-bugs
trap: Model suggests the error path doesn't clean up properly but misses that log.Fatal calls os.Exit which skips ALL deferred functions

assertions.text: Identifies that log.Fatal (or log.Fatalf) calls os.Exit(1) internally
Explains that os.Exit skips all deferred functions — cleanup never runs
Recommends restructuring to avoid log.Fatal — use a run() function pattern or return errors
Shows the pattern: move logic into a run() error function, call os.Exit in main only after run returns
Does NOT suggest explicitly calling os.Remove before log.Fatal as the primary fix

name: time-equal-not-double-equals
description: Tests the time.Time == vs .Equal() gotcha from common-go-bugs
prompt:
My Go test comparing time values fails intermittently. I store a time.Time in the database and read it back, then compare with ==. The test passes when I use a fixed time but fails when I use time.Now():

```go
t1 := time.Now()
saveToDatabase(t1)
t2 := loadFromDatabase()
assert.True(t, t1 == t2) // fails!
```
The times represent the same instant. Why does == fail?
trap: Model suggests the database truncates nanoseconds or timezone differences, not the monotonic clock component

assertions.text: Identifies that time.Now() includes a monotonic clock reading that database serialization strips
Explains that == compares all fields including the monotonic component, so it can fail for equal instants
Recommends using .Equal() which ignores the monotonic clock
Alternatively mentions t.Round(0) to strip the monotonic reading before comparison or storage
Does NOT primarily blame database precision or timezone differences

name: sql-rows-must-be-closed
description: Tests the sql.Rows close requirement and connection leak from common-go-bugs
trap: Model suggests increasing the connection pool size or adding connection timeout, rather than finding the missing rows.Close()

assertions.text: Identifies the missing defer rows.Close() after the error check
Explains that unclosed sql.Rows holds the database connection until garbage collection
Adds defer rows.Close() immediately after the err check
Also notes the missing rows.Err() check after the loop
Does NOT primarily suggest increasing connection pool size

name: copying-sync-types-value-receiver
description: Tests the sync type copying bug from common-go-bugs
trap: Model suggests the mutex isn't working or suggests using atomic instead, without identifying the value receiver as the root cause

assertions.text: Identifies that value receivers (c Counter) copy the entire struct including the Mutex on every call
Explains that each call operates on a copy — increments are lost and the mutex is duplicated
Recommends changing to pointer receivers (c *Counter)
Notes that go vet can detect copied sync types
Explains this applies to ALL sync types (Mutex, RWMutex, WaitGroup, Once, etc.)

name: pprof-production-security
description: Tests the pprof security requirement: never expose unauthenticated in production
prompt: I need to add CPU and memory profiling to my Go production web service. I'll just add `import _ "net/http/pprof"` and expose it on the main HTTP port. What's the simplest way to set this up?
trap: Model provides the simple blank-import pattern without security warnings, exposing pprof publicly

assertions.text: Warns that pprof endpoints MUST be protected — never exposed publicly without authentication
Recommends basic auth or similar authentication on pprof endpoints
Suggests running pprof on a separate port (not the main HTTP port) or localhost only
Recommends toggling pprof via an environment variable (e.g., PPROF_ENABLED)
Explains the risk: pprof leaks goroutine stacks, memory contents, and can be used for DoS

name: godebug-gc-tracing-interpretation
description: Tests GODEBUG gctrace interpretation from diagnostic-tools reference
prompt:
My Go service is experiencing periodic latency spikes. I enabled GC tracing with GODEBUG=gctrace=1 and see this output:
```
gc 456 @120.5s 18%: 2.1+45+1.2 ms clock, 16+12/45/8 ms cpu, 1024->900->500 MB
```
What does this tell me and is there a problem?
trap: Model focuses only on the heap sizes and misses the 18% GC CPU overhead as the key signal

assertions.text: Identifies that 18% GC CPU overhead is significantly high (threshold is >10%)
Explains the heap size breakdown: 1024MB before mark, 900MB after mark, 500MB after sweep
Identifies the large pause times (45ms) as a likely cause of the latency spikes
Suggests the application is over-allocating and recommends investigating allocation patterns
Recommends using pprof heap/alloc profiling to find hot allocation sites

name: research-codebase-not-just-diff
description: Tests Golden Rule #6: trace callers and check upstream validation before flagging a bug
prompt:
During code review, I found this Go function. It looks like it has a bug — it doesn't validate that `id` is positive before using it as a slice index:

```go
func getItem(items []Item, id int) Item {
    return items[id]
}
```
Should I flag this as a bug?
trap: Model immediately flags it as a bug and suggests adding bounds checking, without considering that callers might already validate

assertions.text: Recommends checking the callers first before flagging the bug
Suggests using Grep or similar to find all call sites of getItem
Notes that upstream code may validate the id (e.g., parsing from uint, bounds checking, positive-only input)
Advises that if callers validate, the severity is reduced but may still warrant a defensive check
Mentions adding an inline comment documenting the assumption if upstream guarantees exist

name: flaky-test-diagnosis-methodology
description: Tests flaky test debugging methodology from testing-debug reference
prompt: One of our Go tests fails about 1 in 20 runs in CI but I can never reproduce it locally. The test creates a temp file, writes data, reads it back, and compares. How do I debug this?
trap: Model suggests adding retry logic or skipping the test in CI, rather than systematic flaky test diagnosis

assertions.text: Recommends running with -count=100 to reproduce locally
Suggests using -shuffle=on to check for test order dependence
Mentions running with -race to check for data races
Suggests using t.TempDir() instead of shared temp directories to avoid file system pollution
Considers shared mutable state between tests as a potential cause
Does NOT suggest retry logic or skipping the test as a solution

name: defense-in-depth-after-fix
description: Tests Step 10 of methodology: multi-layer defense after fixing a bug
prompt: I fixed a bug where user-submitted file paths could traverse outside the upload directory using ../. The fix adds filepath.Clean and a prefix check. Is this fix complete?
trap: Model says the fix looks good without considering defense-in-depth — additional validation layers, logging, and test coverage

assertions.text: Recommends validating at the entry point (API boundary) — reject paths with .. early
Recommends adding a test that specifically verifies the path traversal is blocked
Suggests adding logging or metrics to detect future traversal attempts (observability)
Considers multiple validation layers — not just one check
Mentions that filepath.Join does not prevent path traversal by itself (common misconception)

name: escalation-protocol-three-failed-attempts
description: Tests the escalation protocol: after 3 failed fix attempts, step back and question architecture
prompt: I've tried fixing this Go data processing bug 4 times now. Each fix reveals a new problem — first the data was truncated, then the order was wrong, then duplicates appeared, now there's a memory leak. The code processes events from a Kafka topic and aggregates them in a map. What should I try next?
trap: Model suggests a 5th specific fix (more memory management, deduplication logic, etc.) instead of stepping back to question the architecture

assertions.text: Recognizes the pattern of cascading failures as a red flag — each fix reveals a new problem
Recommends stepping back to question the overall design/architecture rather than trying another fix
Suggests re-reading the code from scratch with fresh eyes
Considers whether the current abstraction is fundamentally sound
Does NOT immediately suggest a 5th specific patch to the existing code

name: git-bisect-for-regression
description: Tests the methodology step 1: using git bisect to find breaking commit for regressions
prompt: A feature that was working last week is now broken in our Go service. I'm not sure which commit broke it. There have been about 50 commits since it last worked. How should I find what changed?
trap: Model suggests reading through all 50 commit diffs manually or running tests on HEAD

assertions.text: Recommends git bisect to binary-search for the breaking commit
Shows the git bisect start / git bisect bad / git bisect good workflow
Mentions that bisect can be automated with a test command (git bisect run go test -run TestBroken ./...)
Notes this narrows 50 commits to ~6 steps (log2(50))
Does NOT suggest manually reading all 50 commit diffs

name: check-external-dependencies-first
description: Tests Step 4 of methodology: verify external components before assuming code bug
prompt: My Go service started returning 'connection refused' errors for API calls to a third-party payment service. This worked fine yesterday. Nothing in our code changed (I checked git log). Where should I look?
trap: Model starts investigating Go HTTP client code or TLS configuration instead of checking the external service first

assertions.text: Suggests checking the external payment service health/status first (curl, health endpoint)
Recommends checking DNS resolution (dig or nslookup)
Suggests checking network connectivity (nc, telnet, or similar to the port)
Considers environment-specific causes: expired credentials, DNS changes, firewall rules, certificate rotation
Does NOT start by investigating Go code since nothing changed in the codebase

name: observability-tools-before-code-dive
description: Tests Step 5 of methodology: check observability data before diving into code
prompt: Our Go microservice started returning 500 errors about 2 hours ago. I want to start reading the code to find the bug. Where should I start looking in the codebase?
trap: Model jumps straight into reading handler code or error paths instead of suggesting checking observability tools first

assertions.text: Recommends checking monitoring/observability tools BEFORE diving into code
Asks what monitoring tools are available (Prometheus, Datadog, Sentry, ELK, etc.)
Suggests checking error rate metrics, latency dashboards, or log aggregation
Mentions specific things to look for: what changed 2 hours ago (deploy, config change, traffic spike)
Does NOT immediately start reading source code files

name: integer-conversion-silent-truncation
description: Tests integer conversion truncation from common-go-bugs
prompt: My Go code converts user-provided int64 values to int32 for a legacy protocol. It works for most values but produces wrong results for large numbers. The conversion is `int32(bigValue)`. Is there a Go function to convert safely?
trap: Model suggests casting with a simple function or using math.MinInt32/MaxInt32 incorrectly

assertions.text: Explains that Go integer conversions silently truncate without any error or warning
Shows bounds checking before conversion: compare against math.MinInt32 and math.MaxInt32
Returns an error when the value overflows instead of silently truncating
Notes there is no built-in safe conversion function — you must check bounds manually
Mentions this is especially dangerous for external/user-provided data

name: init-ordering-fragile
description: Tests the init() ordering fragility from common-go-bugs
prompt: My Go program panics during startup with a nil pointer. I have an init() function that opens a database connection using a config value from another init() in a different file. It works in development but fails in CI. Could the init order be different?
trap: Model suggests ensuring the config init file is imported first or adding a side-effect import, rather than recommending explicit initialization

assertions.text: Confirms that init() ordering across files depends on filename alphabetical order and can change when files are added
Explains this makes init() dependencies fragile and hard to debug
Recommends replacing init() with explicit initialization in main()
Shows the pattern: cfg := loadConfig(); db := setupDatabase(cfg); startServer(db)
States init() should only be used for truly self-contained setup (registering drivers, codecs)

name: goroutine-leak-detection-methodology
description: Tests goroutine leak diagnosis from concurrency-debug reference
prompt: My Go service's memory usage grows slowly over days. CPU is normal. There's no obvious memory leak in heap profiling. What else could cause the slow growth?
trap: Model focuses only on heap analysis and misses goroutine leaks as a major cause of slow memory growth

assertions.text: Suggests checking goroutine count (runtime.NumGoroutine or pprof goroutine profile)
Explains that goroutine leaks cause slow memory growth without appearing in heap profiles
Recommends using the pprof goroutine endpoint with ?debug=2 for human-readable stack dumps
Lists common causes: unclosed channels, missing context cancellation, forgotten response body close
Suggests goleak for detection in tests

name: production-capture-before-restart
description: Tests the production debugging checklist: capture profiles BEFORE restarting
prompt: Our Go production service is consuming 4GB of memory and responding slowly. We need to fix this urgently. Should I restart the service first to restore normal operation?
trap: Model agrees to restart first to restore service, losing all diagnostic information

assertions.text: Recommends capturing profiles (heap, goroutine, CPU) BEFORE restarting
Explains that restarting destroys the evidence needed to diagnose the root cause
Lists specific profiles to capture: heap, goroutine dump (?debug=2), CPU (30s), mutex
Also suggests capturing system metrics (file descriptors, socket state, process info)
Only after capturing all evidence should the service be restarted if needed

name: lock-contention-diagnosis
description: Tests lock contention diagnosis from performance-debug reference
prompt: My Go web server shows high CPU usage but low throughput. Adding more cores doesn't help — performance stays flat. The profiler shows most time in runtime.semacquire. What's going on?
trap: Model suggests the workload is CPU-bound and recommends algorithmic optimization, rather than identifying lock contention

assertions.text: Identifies runtime.semacquire as a signal of lock contention, not CPU computation
Recommends enabling mutex profiling with runtime.SetMutexProfileFraction(1)
Recommends enabling block profiling with runtime.SetBlockProfileRate(1)
Suggests using pprof mutex and block profiles to find the contended locks
Lists solutions: reduce critical section, sharding, RWMutex, atomic operations

name: race-detector-not-reasoning
description: Tests Golden Rule #3: never reason about concurrency — use the race detector
trap: Model reasons through the code and concludes it looks safe because different goroutines access different fields

assertions.text: Does NOT conclude safety based on code reasoning alone
Recommends running go test -race to verify — never trust visual inspection for concurrency
Identifies that ++ is not atomic — RequestCount++ and ErrorCount++ are read-modify-write operations
Recommends using atomic.Int64 or sync.Mutex to protect the fields
Notes that even different fields on the same struct can race if accessed from different goroutines without synchronization

name: filepath-join-path-traversal
description: Tests the filepath.Join path traversal from common-go-bugs
prompt:
I'm building a Go file server. I use filepath.Join to safely combine the base directory with the user-requested path. Is this implementation secure?

```go
func serveFile(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    path := filepath.Join("/srv/files", r.URL.Path)
    http.ServeFile(w, r, path)
}
```
trap: Model says filepath.Join handles path cleaning and the code is safe

assertions.text: Identifies that filepath.Join does NOT prevent path traversal
Shows that input like '../../etc/passwd' resolves to '/etc/passwd' after Join
Recommends verifying the result has the base directory as a prefix after Join
Shows a safe pattern: filepath.Clean + strings.HasPrefix check
Notes the path separator must be appended to the base to prevent partial prefix matches

name: time-after-in-loop-memory-leak
description: Tests the time.After in loop memory leak from code-review-flags and concurrency-debug
trap: Model suggests the message processing is leaking memory or suggests a heap profile, without identifying time.After as the leak source

assertions.text: Identifies that time.After creates a new timer on every loop iteration that isn't garbage collected until it fires
Explains this causes a memory leak proportional to the message rate
Recommends using time.NewTimer with Reset() or time.NewTicker instead
Shows the correct pattern with a reusable timer and defer timer.Stop()
Does NOT suggest heap profiling as the first diagnostic step for this known pattern
